answer:
**Fichte's *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit* (1793)** adopts a highly polemical, oratorical posture, framed as a public "speech" directly confronting Europe's princes while rallying "you peoples" against the suppression of thought-freedom (*Denkfreiheit*).[^1] [^2] Its tone is urgent and accusatory, portraying princes as misguided despots who presume divine authority over human minds, claiming not benevolence but *justice*—protection of inalienable rights derived from the moral law and social contract.[^3] Fichte invokes revolutionary peril if freedom is curtailed, warning that suppressed enlightenment leads to violent upheavals, as seen in recent spectacles.[^4] He philosophically grounds thought-freedom as essential to personhood, inseparable from free will and moral agency, rejecting any princely right to censor communication as a violation of humanity itself.[^5] The rhetoric escalates emotionally ("Shout it... into your princes' ears"), blending logical deduction (inalienable rights via contract theory) with vivid appeals to conscience, positioning princes as equals under truth rather than gods.[^6] This direct, defiant address—framed against absolutist censorship—aims to shame rulers into passivity on enlightenment while empowering the public.[^7]

**Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I* (1824)** defends liberty through a systematic, observational treatise, defining it empirically as *power* acquired by overcoming natural obstacles via industry (*industrie*), morality, and societal progress—not a direct assault on princes but a critique of societal flaws producing flawed governments.[^8] [^9] His posture is analytical and constructive, rejecting dogmatic "rights" talk (e.g., Rousseau, Bentham) for cause-effect analysis: liberty expands with skill, virtue, and peaceful production, contracting under ignorance, vice, or violence.[^10] Addressing publicists and readers methodically, Dunoyer examines liberty across societal stages (savage to industrial), arguing governments reflect populations' moral-industrial capacity; true reform lies in mass enlightenment, not institutional tweaks.[^11] The tone is measured, philanthropic yet firm—warning against illusions like "state of nature" freedom or equality fantasies—emphasizing inevitable inequalities under industry but greater overall liberty than violent regimes.[^12] Rhetoric prioritizes explanation over exhortation, using history, economics (Say, Malthus), and logic to show liberty as civilization's *result*, not innate gift.[^13]

**Rhetorical Comparison**: Fichte's posture is confrontational and idealistic, a fiery pamphlet weaponizing moral philosophy against princely overreach, evoking Enlightenment radicalism (rights as divine sparks, anti-paternalism).[^14] He personalizes the foe (princes as "not our God"), risks direct peril, and prioritizes *thought*-liberty as foundational. Dunoyer, writing post-Revolution amid industrial stirrings, is pragmatic and holistic, embedding liberty in socio-economic evolution; governments are symptoms, not villains—society self-reforms via industry/morality.[^15] Fichte demands *immediate* restitution from rulers; Dunoyer charts *gradual* progress, critiquing violence (including revolutions) as self-defeating.[^16] Both elevate liberty beyond state grant—Fichte via inalienable reason, Dunoyer via empirical capacity—but Fichte's is prophetic oratory for crisis, Dunoyer's encyclopedic science for long-term reform. Corpus nearest-neighbor stats link them via shared liberty themes, yet Fichte's urgency contrasts Dunoyer's patience.[^17]

[^1]: Fichte, *Zurückforderung...*, ch.1, [translation/](works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., ch.2, [p-b77ba3](works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-b77ba3).

[^3]: Ibid., ch.1, [p-657cba](works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-657cba); ch.2, [p-d9dcfb](works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-d9dcfb).

[^4]: Ibid., ch.1, [p-47d2fc](works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-47d2fc).

[^5]: Ibid., ch.2, [p-e62dae](works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-e62dae).

[^6]: Ibid., ch.1, [p-aef377](works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-aef377).

[^7]: Ibid., ch.2, [p-f8a6dd](works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-f8a6dd).

[^8]: Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité... VOL I*, ch.0, [translation/](works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/).

[^9]: Ibid., ch.1, [p-341c27](works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-341c27).

[^10]: Ibid., ch.1, [p-fc8999](works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-fc8999).

[^11]: Ibid., ch.0, [p-951a86](works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-951a86).

[^12]: Ibid., ch.12, [translation/](works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/12-ii-obstacles-qui-sopposent-encore-a-la-liberte-dan/translation/).

[^13]: Ibid., ch.1, [p-89dba3](works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-89dba3).

[^14]: Fichte, ch.2, [p-44e1de](works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-44e1de).

[^15]: Dunoyer, ch.0, [p-c4d359](works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-c4d359).

[^17]: Wiki cards and *compare_works*/*find_related* results.
